title: THE SUPPLY SKIFF
subtitle: Bayou Lighthouse · 4:47 PM Thursday · the monthly delivery
epigraph_upright: The Hermit is not alone. The Hermit is provisioned. Once a month the world rows out to prove the difference.
epigraph_reversed: The list you gave them last month was written by someone with more hope than you have now. The skiff brings that person's groceries.
scene_description: The skiff is a dot, then a boat, then Jules and a boy you don't know shipping oars at your pier with a month of cans and the mail. The light doesn't need you until 6:40. The manifest needs checking, the boy needs somewhere to put his eyes, and Jules — Jules has been rowing out here eleven years and has never once come up the stairs. There is an hour and a half of daylight and three people on a pier built for one.
direction_hint: Check the manifest. Learn the boy's name. The lamp still gets tended at 6:40 no matter who is on the pier — connect three, keep Inertia under 7, and be somewhere that counts as a threshold when the skiff pushes off.
opening_log_lines: Oarlocks, regular as a clock that likes its work.
Click [b]PHASE: ACTION[/b] for the rules. TEND LAMP has a window tonight — don't drift past it.
There's an envelope in the mail sack that isn't a bill. You can see it from here.

inertia_thresholds.label: the logbook page wants more on it
you have not heard a human voice tonight
the bunk room is bigger than it was
the lamp could be tended later
you forget which hour it is
the watch was not, this time, kept

thresholds.label: DOOR
thresholds.flavor: Out to the cypress-pile platform. The pier under your feet has been here longer than you have.

thresholds.label: LENS HATCH
thresholds.flavor: Up. The lens stage above the hatch. Brass and glass and ninety feet of bayou wind.

notes: Fourth setup (Wave-3, 2026-07-19) · mid-tier at a distinct hour per the bookend pattern. Mechanical scaffolding inherited from the arcana's medium sibling; functional win/loss contract is the generic evaluator (connections + inertia); named keys are the scenario's narrative contract.

scenario_visitors.name: Rémy (Jules's sister's boy, first run out)
scenario_visitors.lore_text: Rémy. Fourteen. Jules's sister's boy, rowing the supply run for the first time because Jules's shoulder is not what it was and nobody has said so out loud. Has never seen a Fresnel lens. Has been told not to ask to.
scenario_visitors.tutorial_note: Rémy connects through WAIT at the pier — he will ask about the light if you give him enough silence to ask in. Then LOG the visit; he should be in the book.

steps.greet: He hands up cans two at a time, careful as church. He says nothing that isn't a number off the manifest.
steps.listen: Given room, he asks how far the light reaches. Then, quieter, whether you ever get used to the fog horn. Then — the real question — whether it's lonely, asked in the way of someone hoping the answer is no and suspecting it's complicated.
steps.deliver: You take him up to see the lens. Jules stays with the skiff. That's not an accident and all three of you know it.
steps.sit_with: At the top he doesn't touch anything. He stands where the light will stand at 6:40 and looks at the water the way you did, the first month, before it was work.

scenario_visitors.name: the manifest, two items amended in pencil
scenario_visitors.lore_text: The manifest is typed at the co-op, but two lines are amended in Jules's pencil: coffee doubled, and a tin of the biscuits you mentioned once, in March, in passing, to nobody in particular you thought.
scenario_visitors.tutorial_note: The manifest connects through LOG — enter the delivery, and enter the amendments, because the amendments are the part the log is FOR.

steps.greet: It's clipped to the crate. The pencil lines are neater than the type.
steps.listen: Read in order, it's groceries. Read twice, it's eleven years of someone noticing what runs out early.
steps.deliver: You sign the bottom copy and hand it back. Under your name you write 'both received' — Jules will know which both.
steps.sit_with: The biscuit tin goes on the shelf where the old keeper kept his. Some shelves have jobs.
